thread.title: Lead by example

title: Lead by example
text:
Congratulations to our reelected local state representatives serving Snyder, Union, and Northumberland counties: David Rowe, Stephanie Borowicz, Joanne Stehr and Michael Stender. These Republican legislators campaigned proudly on reducing big government, eliminating wasteful spending of taxpayer dollars and championing fiscal conservatism.
As they return to Harrisburg, I call upon these fiscal conservatives to demonstrate that these principles are more than just campaign promises by adhering to the core values they claim to uphold, fulfilling their commitment to fiscal conservatism, and addressing one of Pennsylvania’s most glaring inefficiencies: our bloated, career politician-driven, full-time Legislature.
Pennsylvania has the dubious honor of having the largest full-time legislature in the United States with 253 members in the House and Senate. Our Commonwealth’s taxpayers pay our politicians more than any other state except New York and California. Our commonwealth also has the distinction of being only 1 of 10 states to continue with a full-time legislature, while 40 states have embraced more efficient, part-time legislatures instead of prioritizing political careers over fiscal responsibility.
Republican leaders such as former House Speaker Sam Smith and Rep. Jerry Knowles have previously sought to address this issue by proposing constitutional amendments to reduce the size of the House from 203 members to 151. Their proposals could save our taxpayers an estimated $15 million annually — yet this common-sense reform was derailed by the very “small government” fiscal conservatives within their own ranks.
What is $15 million a year in savings? It is around the amount Reps. Stehr, Rowe, Stender and Borowicz eliminated from the 2024 Pennsylvania budget.
However, these cuts didn’t target government waste or bureaucratic excess.
Instead, they directly impacted vital and essential services provided to senior citizens across the commonwealth such as home-delivered meals, assistance with personal care, and operation of senior community centers. Instead of older adults getting help, 253 legislators chose to keep their lifetime medical benefits, retirement, per diems, and six-figure salaries — privileges and luxuries that the vast majority of their constituents, including the seniors who lost critical services, can only dream of.
In the time of our forefathers, the legislature was part-time, composed of farmers, bankers, lawyers and teachers who would return to their professions and daily lives to live under the same laws they enacted.
This is no longer the case for these career Harrisburg officeholders.
True fiscal conservatism demands meaningful reform, including term limits, to ensure that politicians passing regulations on small businesses and farmers from their offices in Harrisburg are eventually required to return to the private sector and directly experience the real-world impact of the decisions they made — just as small business owners and farmers must.
Representatives Rowe, Stehr, Borowicz, and Stender have an opportunity to lead by example. Cloaked in their unified fiscal conservatism, they can renew efforts to reduce the size of our exorbitantly expensive Legislature and save taxpayer dollars by reducing the size of our lavishly general assembly, by ending the wasteful spending of taxpayer dollars and ending the era of the career politicians.
Antonio Michetti lives in Herndon.
